matter.title: Insolvent estate of Olivia B. Ellsworth
matter.summary: Theodore B. Kingsley administers the insolvent estate of Olivia B. Ellsworth, whose allowed claims of roughly $379749 far exceed probate assets of about $55959, requiring the court to apply the statutory order of priority among competing creditors and allowances.

fact_pattern.summary: Theodore B. Kingsley administers the insolvent estate of Olivia B. Ellsworth, whose allowed claims of roughly $379749 far exceed probate assets of about $55959, requiring the court to apply the statutory order of priority among competing creditors and allowances.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Olivia B. Ellsworth died on 2025-06-22. Theodore B. Kingsley, the adult child, was appointed to administer the estate and soon discovered that it is insolvent: probate assets total only about $55959, while allowed and asserted claims approach $379749, including roughly $77815 in secured debt.

Because the estate cannot pay everyone, the personal representative must marshal the assets, determine which claims are valid, and pay them in the order of priority set by the Maine Probate Code rather than first-come-first-served. The surviving family asserts homestead, exempt-property, and family allowances that take priority over general creditors, while several unsecured creditors compete for whatever remains.

To protect against personal liability for an improper distribution, Theodore has asked the court to proceed by formal, supervised administration, give notice to creditors, and approve a plan of distribution that abates the devises and prorates the unsecured claims.
undisputed_facts: Asserted claims against the estate exceed its probate assets.
There are at least 5 creditors competing for payment.
disputed_facts: The validity and classification of several creditor claims.
Whether the family allowances were properly claimed and in what amount.
Whether certain transfers before death should be recovered as estate assets.

third_parties.name: Evergreen Partners Co.
third_parties.relevance: Asserts a large unsecured claim for end-of-life care.

third_parties.name: Maine Revenue Services
third_parties.relevance: Asserts a priority claim for unpaid taxes.

third_parties.name: Marcus K. Poe
third_parties.relevance: Stands to receive nothing if the estate remains insolvent.

third_parties.name: Maria T. Coe
third_parties.relevance: Claims the homestead, exempt-property, and family allowances ahead of general creditors.

exchanges.question: What does the estate actually own?
exchanges.answer: Not much liquid. A house with a mortgage, a vehicle, a small bank account. The debts are far bigger than the assets.

exchanges.question: Who is demanding payment?
exchanges.answer: The hospital, a couple of credit cards, the funeral home, the mortgage lender, and a tax notice arrived too. Everyone wants to be paid first.

exchanges.question: Is anyone in the household relying on the estate?
exchanges.answer: Yes. The surviving spouse needs the homestead and family allowance just to get by.

exchanges.question: What worries you most about serving?
exchanges.answer: I do not want to be personally on the hook if I pay the wrong people in the wrong order.

exchanges.question: What outcome are you seeking?
exchanges.answer: Handle it correctly, pay claims in the right priority, protect the family's allowances, and close the estate without liability.

goals: Administer the insolvent estate in the statutory order of priority.
Preserve the surviving family's homestead, exempt-property, and family allowances.
Avoid personal liability for any improper or out-of-order distribution.
priorities: Correct classification and priority of competing claims.
An orderly, court-approved plan of distribution.
desired_outcomes: A court-approved distribution that pays claims in priority and abates devises.
A discharge of the personal representative on closing.
non_starters: Paying general creditors ahead of statutory allowances and priority claims.

constraints.budget: Limited; estate funds are scarce and fees themselves are a priority expense.
constraints.timeline: Bounded by the non-claim period for creditors.

issues.issue: Order of priority for payment of claims against an insolvent estate
sub_questions: Which claims are priority claims (costs of administration, funeral, taxes)?
How are unsecured claims prorated when assets are insufficient?
issues.client_position: Seeks court approval to pay claims strictly in statutory order.

issues.issue: Recovery of non-probate transfers to satisfy claims
sub_questions: Are recent transfers reachable to pay creditors of an insolvent estate?

issues.issue: Abatement of devises where the estate is insolvent
sub_questions: In what order do devises abate to satisfy claims and allowances?

issues.issue: Family, homestead, and exempt-property allowances versus creditors
sub_questions: Do the allowances take priority over general creditors?
issues.client_position: Contends the allowances are paid ahead of general creditors.

expert_opinions.opinion: After priority claims and allowances, unsecured creditors recover only a fraction of their claims.
expert_opinions.basis: Inventory, claims register, and a proposed distribution schedule.

evidence.title: Certified death certificate
evidence.description: Establishes the date of death and opens the non-claim period.

evidence.title: Register of creditor claims
evidence.description: All filed claims with amounts and classifications.

evidence.title: Mortgage payoff statement
evidence.description: Documents the secured claim against the real property.
